title: Registry Generic Type-Architecture: Class-`Key` vs Method-`get<Key>`
summary: Prior-art grounding backlog #582: the FU and WE plugs trees shipped mutually-incompatible public type contracts on three core registry surfaces (CustomContext, CustomTextNodeRegistry, CustomElementRegistry); #580 converges them into one shared runtime, which can carry only one form. The real divergence is in CustomContext.ts (the base class implementing Registry) — CustomContextRegistry.ts is byte-identical between repos. WE puts a class-level Key type param on CustomContext (defaulted to keyof ContextValue, and never overridden by any consumer in the workspace — it is dead) and mistypes the Registry Definition slot as the whole record, omitting values()/entries() and returning void from delete(); FU uses a single ContextValue param with a method-level get<Key extends keyof ContextValue>, conforms to Registry<value, string> correctly, and implements the full Map-shaped surface (values/entries/delete:boolean). The platform Map<K,V> + iterator protocol is the vocabulary the codebase's own Registry interface mirrors. Default A: adopt FU's method-level-generic string-keyed Map-shaped form — native-first, the only Registry-conformant side, and zero-consumer-cost. Riders: adopt FU's typings (ImplementedTextNode<any>; concrete upgrade(root:Node); options? optional) and port WE's ensureNativelyConstructible runtime up as additive (not a fork).
